title: Past, Future, and Conditional
description: Compare the major Bulgarian tense builders and learn how time is marked in real sentences.

content.title: Three common time builders
content.description: Bulgarian often uses a helper word plus a verb form to show time.

rows: talks about completed or historical events
shows a background or repeated past action

content.title: Do not translate too literally
content.text: English tense labels do not always match Bulgarian forms one-to-one. Focus on the time meaning, then learn the Bulgarian structure that expresses it.

content.title: Future tense
content.description: The future is usually built with ще plus the present-tense form.

content.en: I will read this book tomorrow.
content.note: The helper word ще marks the future.
The main verb stays in its base present pattern.

content.title: Future tense rule
bullets: Keep the verb in the form you would use in the present.
Use context or time words like утре, после, or по-късно to make the meaning clear.

content.title: Past tense and imperfect
content.description: Bulgarian distinguishes a finished past event from a background or repeated past action.

content.en: As a child I used to read many books.
content.note: An ongoing or repeated past habit.

content.title: Past-tense clues
items: Time words like вчера and преди often point to the past.
Completed events usually use the aorist-like form.
Background or repeated actions often use the imperfect.
The verb aspect changes the meaning as much as the tense does.

content.title: Conditional mood
content.description: The conditional helps you talk about imagined, dependent, or polite actions.

content.bg: Щях да тръгна по-рано, но заваля.
content.en: I was going to leave earlier, but it started raining.
content.note: A planned action that did not happen.

content.question: What is the most common marker for the Bulgarian future tense?
content.note: It appears before the present-tense verb form.
